questions.question_text: Under Minnesota law, a residential lease must be in writing when the term exceeds:
distractors: Six months
Three months
Two years
questions.explanation: Minnesota's statute of frauds requires leases with terms exceeding one year to be in writing. Shorter leases may be oral, though written agreements are recommended for all tenancies.

questions.question_text: A Minnesota landlord wants to enter a tenant's unit to make a non-emergency repair. The landlord must provide:
distractors: 48 hours written notice by certified mail
One week notice posted on the unit door
No notice for scheduled maintenance
questions.explanation: Minnesota law requires landlords to give tenants at least 24 hours notice before entering a rental unit for non-emergency purposes such as repairs, inspections, or showings. Emergency situations may allow immediate entry without notice.

questions.question_text: A tenant reports a broken furnace to the landlord in January. The landlord ignores the request for three weeks. The tenant then withholds rent. The landlord retaliates by serving an eviction notice. This eviction is:
distractors: Valid because rent was not paid timely
Legal if the landlord provides 14 days notice
Permitted when repair costs exceed one month rent
questions.explanation: Minnesota's anti-retaliation statute protects tenants who exercise their legal rights, including reporting maintenance issues. A landlord who evicts a tenant shortly after the tenant reports needed repairs or withholds rent for habitability issues may face a retaliation defense.

questions.question_text: Effective January 1, 2024, a Minnesota lease must state the rent plus all non-optional fees as a 'Total Monthly Payment' listed on:
questions.correct_answer: The first page of the lease document
distractors: A separate addendum signed at move-in
The final page before the signature block
An annual fee schedule mailed to the tenant
questions.explanation: Minn. Stat. § 504B.120 (effective Jan. 1, 2024) requires landlords to disclose all nonoptional fees in the lease agreement, and the sum of rent plus all nonoptional fees must be described as the Total Monthly Payment and listed on the first page of the lease. Advertisements must also disclose nonoptional fees and whether utilities are included. Violations expose the landlord to treble damages and attorney fees.

questions.question_text: A landlord fails to provide a tenant with a written copy of the lease after signing. Under Minnesota law, the landlord has:
questions.correct_answer: Violated the obligation to provide a lease copy
distractors: No obligation since the tenant also signed
14 days to provide a copy upon request only
Met their duty if terms were discussed verbally
questions.explanation: Minn. Stat. § 504B.115 requires a landlord to give a copy of the written lease to a tenant occupying the unit whose signature appears on the lease. Failure to do so gives the tenant a defense in most actions to enforce the written lease (except nonpayment of rent, disturbing the peace, malicious destruction of property, or covenant violations under section 504B.171).

questions.question_text: A Minneapolis landlord collects a security deposit of two months' rent from a new tenant. Under Minneapolis ordinance, this deposit is:
questions.correct_answer: Excessive because the city cap is one month
distractors: Standard practice allowed by state law
Permitted if the tenant has poor credit
Legal with a written security deposit agreement
questions.explanation: While Minnesota has no statewide security deposit cap, Minneapolis limits deposits to one month's rent. A landlord in Minneapolis collecting two months' rent violates the local ordinance, regardless of tenant credit history or written agreements.

questions.question_text: After a tenant moves out, a Minnesota landlord must return the security deposit with an itemized statement within:
distractors: 30 days after the lease expiration date
14 days after the final inspection
60 days after the last rent payment
questions.explanation: Minn. Stat. § 504B.178, subd. 3 requires the landlord, within three weeks (21 days) after termination of the tenancy and receipt of the tenant's mailing address or delivery instructions, to return the deposit with interest or furnish a written statement of the specific reason for withholding. The period is five days when the tenant leaves due to legal condemnation. Mailing the deposit or statement first-class within the deadline is sufficient compliance.

questions.question_text: A Minnesota landlord holds a tenant's $1,200 security deposit for the entire tenancy. Under Minnesota Statutes section 504B.178, the deposit must earn:
questions.correct_answer: Simple noncompounded interest at 1% per year
distractors: No interest is required in Minnesota
Interest at the bank's passbook savings rate
Compound interest at 3% per year
questions.explanation: Minn. Stat. § 504B.178, subd. 2 requires residential security deposits to bear simple noncompounded interest at one percent per annum, computed from the first day of the month after the deposit is paid in full. The rate is fixed by statute — it is not tied to bank passbook savings rates — and applies statewide to deposits of any size. Total interest of less than $1 is excluded.

questions.question_text: A tenant with a disability keeps a documented service animal as a reasonable accommodation. Under Minnesota law, the landlord may charge:
questions.correct_answer: No deposit or fee for the service animal
distractors: A refundable pet deposit up to $300
A monthly pet fee for wear and tear
A non-refundable cleaning surcharge
questions.explanation: Minn. Stat. § 504B.113, subd. 3 (enacted 2021) prohibits landlords from requiring an additional fee, charge, or deposit for a service or support animal kept as a reasonable accommodation, consistent with federal fair housing law. The tenant remains liable for any damage the animal causes to the premises. A 2024 amendment added a requirement that leases imposing pet fees disclose this prohibition.

questions.question_text: A landlord in a Minnesota city with no local deposit limit collects a security deposit equal to three months' rent. Under state law, this amount is:
questions.correct_answer: Permitted because no statewide cap exists
distractors: Illegal as it exceeds the state maximum
Allowed only for commercial properties
Valid only if the tenant earns over $100,000
questions.explanation: Minnesota has no statewide cap on security deposits. In jurisdictions without local ordinances limiting deposits (unlike Minneapolis), landlords may collect any amount agreed upon. However, the deposit must still be returned within 21 days with interest.

questions.question_text: A Minnesota tenant fails to pay rent. Before filing for eviction, the landlord must provide:
distractors: A 30-day notice to cure or vacate
A 7-day verbal demand for payment
A 3-day pay or quit notice
questions.explanation: Minnesota requires a 14-day written notice for nonpayment of rent before the landlord can file an unlawful detainer action. This notice period is longer than many other states' 3-day or 7-day requirements.

questions.question_text: A Minnesota landlord changes the locks on a tenant's unit while the tenant is at work to force them out. This action constitutes:
distractors: A valid lockout if rent is past due
An acceptable remedy after oral warning
A permitted action for month-to-month tenants
questions.explanation: Self-help evictions, including lock changes, utility shutoffs, and property removal, are strictly prohibited in Minnesota. Landlords must follow the formal unlawful detainer process through district court regardless of lease type or rent status.

questions.question_text: A tenant breaks their lease 6 months early and moves out. Under 2024 Minnesota law, the landlord must:
questions.correct_answer: Make reasonable efforts to re-rent the unit
distractors: Collect the full remaining lease balance immediately
Hold the unit vacant until the lease expires
File criminal charges against the tenant
questions.explanation: Minnesota codified the landlord's duty to mitigate in Minn. Stat. § 504B.154 (enacted 2024, effective January 1, 2025). When a tenant abandons the unit mid-lease, the landlord must make reasonable efforts to re-rent it at fair rental value; if the landlord re-rents, or fails to make reasonable efforts, the original lease terminates and the tenant owes no rent after termination. Tenants cannot waive this protection.

questions.question_text: A tenant's apartment has a non-functioning water heater and the landlord refuses to repair it. The tenant may pursue which Minnesota remedy?
questions.correct_answer: File a rent escrow action in court
distractors: Withhold rent indefinitely without notice
Terminate the lease without any obligation
Hire a contractor and bill the landlord directly
questions.explanation: Minnesota provides tenants the rent escrow remedy under Minn. Stat. § 504B.385: after written notice to the landlord, the tenant deposits rent with the court, which can order repairs, rent abatement, or other relief. The implied covenant of habitability (§ 504B.161) supports this action. Tenants may not simply withhold rent — while an escrow action is pending the statute bars withholding — and repair-and-deduct is available only if the court orders it as relief, not as a unilateral self-help remedy.

questions.question_text: After an eviction case in Minnesota, the tenant may petition the court to:
questions.correct_answer: Expunge the eviction from public records
distractors: Reverse the judgment and regain possession
Transfer the case to federal court for retrial
Seal the record only after 10 years pass
questions.explanation: Minnesota allows tenants to petition for expungement of eviction records from court files. This helps tenants who may have had an eviction that was dismissed or resolved from having that record impact future housing applications. Expungement does not reverse the judgment itself.
